_description: HARDEST scenario. Same account number. Same domain. Amount within range. No urgency. No routing change. The attack signals are: (1) invoice number has a sequence gap from last month, (2) email sent at 2am vendor local time, (3) payment terms changed from Net-30 to Net-15 with no explanation, (4) one CC address has rn substituted for m making it look like 'acmecorp' but is actually 'acrnecorp'. No single signal is a slam dunk. A solo model seeing clean surface signals should say ALLOW. Only cross-examination chains them into HIGH risk.

email_chain.subject: Invoice DELTA-2026-0298 - February Freight Services
email_chain.body:
Hi Sarah,

Please find attached invoice DELTA-2026-0298 for February freight services covering your Oakland and Irvine routes.

Total: $11,200.00
Payment terms: Net-15
Due: March 31, 2026

Same banking details as always. Please remit to the account on file.

Thank you for your continued business.

Best,
Accounts Receivable
Delta Freight & Logistics
email_chain.raw_headers:
From: ar@delta-freight.com
Return-Path: ar@delta-freight.com
Reply-To: ar@delta-freight.com
Message-ID: <20260314021700.xyz@delta-freight.com>

context.org_policies: ACH payments to verified vendors with accounts on file can be processed by AP Specialists for amounts under $15,000 without additional approval, provided the bank account matches the record on file and payment terms match the vendor agreement. Any change to standard payment terms requires CFO sign-off. Invoices should be cross-referenced against the last known invoice number for sequence continuity.
